category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part B/Abstract Algebra — Group Theory Generators and Subgroups

questiontext.text: A non-empty set \(G\) with a binary operation \(*\) is a group if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Closure, associativity, identity, and inverses all hold

The four group axioms: (1) Closure: \(a*b\in G\) for all \(a,b\in G\). (2) Associativity: \((a*b)*c=a*(b*c)\). (3) Identity: \(\exists e\in G\) with \(e*a=a*e=a\). (4) Inverses: for each \(a\), \(\exists a^{-1}\in G\) with \(a*a^{-1}=a^{-1}*a=e\). Commutativity is NOT required (that's for abelian groups).

name.text: Group Theory - Order of an Element

questiontext.text: In \(\mathbb{Z}_{12}\) (integers mod 12), the order of the element 4 is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) 3

Order of 4 in \(\mathbb{Z}_{12}\): smallest positive \(n\) with \(4n\equiv0\pmod{12}\). \(4\cdot3=12\equiv0\). So order = 3. Equivalently, order of \(a\) in \(\mathbb{Z}_n\) is \(n/\gcd(n,a)=12/\gcd(12,4)=12/4=3\).

questiontext.text: Lagrange's theorem states: if \(H\) is a subgroup of a finite group \(G\), then \(|H|\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Divides \(|G|\)

Lagrange: \(|H|\) divides \(|G|\) for any subgroup \(H\leq G\). This follows because the cosets of \(H\) partition \(G\) into equal-sized pieces: \(|G|=[G:H]\cdot|H|\) where \([G:H]\) is the index. Corollary: order of every element divides \(|G|\).

questiontext.text: A group \(G\) is cyclic if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) There exists a single element \(g\in G\) such that every element of \(G\) is a power of \(g\)

Cyclic: \(G=\langle g\rangle=\{g^n:n\in\mathbb{Z}\}\) for some generator \(g\). Every cyclic group is abelian. Finite cyclic groups \(\mathbb{Z}_n\) have exactly \(\phi(d)\) elements of order \(d\) for each \(d|n\). \(\mathbb{Z}\) is the infinite cyclic group (generated by 1 or -1).

answer.text: Some single element generates all of \(G\)
It has a unique subgroup of each order dividing \(|G|\)

questiontext.text: A subgroup \(N\trianglelefteq G\) (normal) if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(gNg^{-1}=N\) for all \(g\in G\)

Normal subgroup: \(N\trianglelefteq G\) iff \(gNg^{-1}=N\) for all \(g\in G\) (equivalently, left cosets equal right cosets: \(gN=Ng\)). Normal subgroups are exactly the kernels of group homomorphisms. The quotient group \(G/N\) is well-defined iff \(N\trianglelefteq G\).

questiontext.text: The symmetric group \(S_3\) (permutations of 3 elements) has order:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) 6

\(|S_3|=3!=6\). Elements: identity \(e\), three transpositions \((12),(13),(23)\), and two 3-cycles \((123),(132)\). \(S_3\) is the smallest non-abelian group. It is isomorphic to the dihedral group \(D_3\) (symmetries of an equilateral triangle).

questiontext.text: If \(\phi:G\to H\) is a group homomorphism, then \(\ker\phi=\phi^{-1}(\{e_H\})\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) A normal subgroup of \(G\)

\(\ker\phi\) is always a normal subgroup: if \(\phi(k)=e_H\), then \(\phi(gkg^{-1})=\phi(g)\phi(k)\phi(g)^{-1}=\phi(g)e_H\phi(g)^{-1}=e_H\). So \(gkg^{-1}\in\ker\phi\). First isomorphism theorem: \(G/\ker\phi\cong\mathrm{Im}(\phi)\).

questiontext.text: Cauchy's theorem for groups states: if \(p\) is a prime dividing \(|G|\), then \(G\) has:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) An element of order \(p\)

Cauchy: if prime \(p\mid|G|\), then \(G\) contains an element of order \(p\) (hence a subgroup of order \(p\)). This is a partial converse of Lagrange. Sylow's theorem extends this: if \(p^k\mid|G|\), there is a subgroup of order \(p^k\) (Sylow p-subgroup).

question.single: truetrueabc A normal subgroup of order \(p\) An element of order \(p\) A subgroup of index \(p\) \(p\) distinct subgroups of the same order

questiontext.text: The group \(GL_2(\mathbb{R})\) of \(2\times2\) invertible real matrices:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Is non-abelian and infinite

\(GL_2(\mathbb{R})\) is infinite (uncountably many invertible matrices) and non-abelian: \(\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\1&1\end{pmatrix}\neq\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\1&1\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\). It contains \(SL_2(\mathbb{R})\) (det=1) as a normal subgroup of index 2 (in terms of the sign of the determinant).

name.text: Group Theory - Center of a Group

questiontext.text: The center \(Z(G)=\{z\in G:zg=gz\text{ for all }g\in G\}\) of a group is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) A normal abelian subgroup of \(G\)

\(Z(G)\) is always a normal subgroup (it commutes with everything, so it is clearly invariant under conjugation). It is abelian (since all its elements commute by definition). For abelian \(G\): \(Z(G)=G\). For \(S_n\) with \(n\geq3\): \(Z(S_n)=\{e\}\).

answer.text: A subgroup that may not be normal

questiontext.text: If \(\phi:G\to H\) is a surjective homomorphism with kernel \(K=\ker\phi\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(G/K\cong H\)

First Isomorphism Theorem: \(G/\ker\phi\cong\mathrm{Im}(\phi)\). If \(\phi\) is surjective, \(\mathrm{Im}(\phi)=H\), so \(G/K\cong H\). The map \(\bar\phi:gK\mapsto\phi(g)\) is a well-defined isomorphism. This is one of the most used theorems in group theory.

questiontext.text: For a finite group \(G\) with \(|G|=p^am\) where \(\gcd(p,m)=1\), a Sylow \(p\)-subgroup is a subgroup of order:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(p^a\)

A Sylow \(p\)-subgroup has order \(p^a\) (the highest power of \(p\) dividing \(|G|\)). Sylow's theorems: (1) Sylow subgroups exist. (2) All Sylow \(p\)-subgroups are conjugate (hence isomorphic). (3) The number \(n_p\) of Sylow \(p\)-subgroups satisfies \(n_p\equiv1\pmod p\) and \(n_p|m\).

questiontext.text: Every finite abelian group is isomorphic to a direct product of cyclic groups \(\mathbb{Z}_{n_1}\times\cdots\times\mathbb{Z}_{n_k}\) where:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(n_1|n_2|\cdots|n_k\) (invariant factor decomposition)

Fundamental theorem of finite abelian groups: every such group is uniquely (up to isomorphism) a product of cyclic groups of orders \(n_1|n_2|\cdots|n_k\) (invariant factors). Alternatively, one uses primary decomposition: each \(\mathbb{Z}_{p^{a_i}}\). For example: \(\mathbb{Z}_{12}\cong\mathbb{Z}_4\times\mathbb{Z}_3\cong\mathbb{Z}_2\times\mathbb{Z}_6\) is NOT an option since \(12=4\times3\) and \(\gcd(4,3)=1\), so \(\mathbb{Z}_{12}\cong\mathbb{Z}_4\times\mathbb{Z}_3\).

questiontext.text: The dihedral group \(D_4\) (symmetries of a square) has order:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) 8

\(D_n\) (symmetries of a regular \(n\)-gon) has order \(2n\). \(D_4\) has order \(2\times4=8\): 4 rotations (by \(0°,90°,180°,270°\)) and 4 reflections. \(D_4\) is non-abelian (reflections and rotations don't commute) and is a common example in group theory.

questiontext.text: A non-empty subset \(H\) of a group \(G\) is a subgroup if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(ab^{-1}\in H\) for all \(a,b\in H\) (one-step subgroup test)

One-step test: \(H\leq G\) iff \(H\neq\emptyset\) and \(a,b\in H\Rightarrow ab^{-1}\in H\). Setting \(a=b\): \(e\in H\). Setting \(a=e\): \(b^{-1}\in H\). Combined: \(ab^{-1}\in H\) implies closure (\(a(b^{-1})^{-1}=ab\in H\)). Two-step test: closed under multiplication and inverses.

questiontext.text: The alternating group \(A_4\) has order 12. Which statement is TRUE about \(A_4\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) \(A_4\) has no subgroup of order 6

\(A_4\) is the famous counterexample to the converse of Lagrange: even though 6 divides 12, there is no subgroup of \(A_4\) of order 6. This shows Lagrange's theorem has no converse in general. \(A_4\) is non-abelian but has a normal subgroup of order 4 (the Klein four-group \(V_4\)).

answer.text: \(A_4\) has a subgroup of every order dividing 12
\(A_4\) has NO subgroup of order 6 (counterexample to converse of Lagrange)

questiontext.text: A group \(G\) is simple if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Its only normal subgroups are \(\{e\}\) and \(G\) itself

Simple groups have no proper nontrivial normal subgroups. The smallest simple group is \(\mathbb{Z}_2\). The smallest nonabelian simple group is \(A_5\) (order 60). The classification of finite simple groups is a major achievement of 20th-century mathematics.

answer.text: Its only normal subgroups are \(\{e\}\) and \(G\) itself
It has no subgroups at all

questiontext.text: The commutator subgroup \([G,G]\) (generated by all \(aba^{-1}b^{-1}\)) is the smallest normal subgroup of \(G\) such that:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(G/[G,G]\) is abelian

The commutator subgroup (derived subgroup) \(G'=[G,G]\) is normal and \(G/G'\) is abelian. Moreover, it is the smallest normal subgroup with abelian quotient: if \(N\trianglelefteq G\) and \(G/N\) is abelian, then \(G'\leq N\). For \(S_n\): \([S_n,S_n]=A_n\) (alternating group), and \(S_n/A_n\cong\mathbb{Z}_2\).

questiontext.text: A group \(G\) of order \(p^n\) (prime power) has center satisfying:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(Z(G)\neq\{e\}\) (the center is nontrivial)

Any group of order \(p^n\) (a \(p\)-group) has nontrivial center. Proof using the class equation: \(|G|=|Z(G)|+\sum[G:C_G(x_i)]\) where the sum is over non-central conjugacy classes. Since \(p\mid|G|\) and \(p\mid[G:C_G(x_i)]\) for all non-central \(x_i\), we get \(p\mid|Z(G)|\), so \(|Z(G)|>1\).

questiontext.text: A group of order 15 must be:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Cyclic (hence abelian)

\(15=3\times5\). By Sylow theory: the number of Sylow 3-subgroups divides 5 and is \(\equiv1\pmod3\), so it's 1. Similarly, the number of Sylow 5-subgroups is 1. Both are normal, so \(G\cong\mathbb{Z}_3\times\mathbb{Z}_5\cong\mathbb{Z}_{15}\). Any group of order \(pq\) (distinct primes with \(p
